Real Analysis

Continuous Functions on Intervals

Continuous Functions on Intervals

Concept Check

Question 1: The Recursion x1=14, xn+1=xn2+14x_1 = \tfrac14,\ x_{n+1} = x_n^2 + \tfrac14x1​=41​, xn+1​=xn2​+41​

We analyze this sequence two ways — first via Monotone Convergence (Lecture 8), then by checking whether the Contractive Sequence criterion (Lecture 12) also applies, which turns out to be instructive precisely because it fails.

Method 1: Monotone Convergence

Step 1: The sequence is increasing. First check the base case:

x2=(14)2+14=516>14=x1.x_2 = \left(\frac14\right)^2 + \frac14 = \frac{5}{16} > \frac14 = x_1.x2​=(41​)2+41​=165​>41​=x1​.

For the inductive step, suppose xk<xk+1x_k < x_{k+1}xk​<xk+1​. Then

xk+2−xk+1=(xk+12+14)−(xk2+14)=xk+12−xk2=(xk+1−xk)(xk+1+xk).x_{k+2} - x_{k+1} = \left(x_{k+1}^2 + \frac14\right) - \left(x_k^2 + \frac14\right) = x_{k+1}^2 - x_k^2 = (x_{k+1}-x_k)(x_{k+1}+x_k).xk+2​−xk+1​=(xk+12​+41​)−(xk2​+41​)=xk+12​−xk2​=(xk+1​−xk​)(xk+1​+xk​).

Since xk+1>xkx_{k+1} > x_kxk+1​>xk​ by the inductive hypothesis, and both terms are positive (shown in Step 2 below), this product is positive, so xk+2>xk+1x_{k+2} > x_{k+1}xk+2​>xk+1​. By induction, (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is increasing.

Step 2: The sequence is bounded above by 12\tfrac1221​. Base case: x1=14<12x_1 = \tfrac14 < \tfrac12x1​=41​<21​. Inductive step: suppose xk<12x_k < \tfrac12xk​<21​. Then xk2<14x_k^2 < \tfrac14xk2​<41​, so

xk+1=xk2+14<14+14=12.x_{k+1} = x_k^2 + \frac14 < \frac14 + \frac14 = \frac12.xk+1​=xk2​+41​<41​+41​=21​.

By induction, xn<12x_n < \tfrac12xn​<21​ for all nnn.

Step 3: Apply Monotone Convergence and solve for the limit. Since (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is increasing and bounded above, it converges by the Monotone Convergence Theorem (Lecture 8). Let x=lim⁡xnx = \lim x_nx=limxn​. Passing to the limit in the recursion,

x=x2+14⟹x2−x+14=0⟹(x−12)2=0,x = x^2 + \frac14 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x^2 - x + \frac14 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \left(x - \frac12\right)^2 = 0,x=x2+41​⟹x2−x+41​=0⟹(x−21​)2=0,

so x=12x = \tfrac12x=21​.

Method 2: Why the Contraction Approach Fails

It is tempting to try showing (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is contractive, following the template of Lecture 12. Expanding consecutive differences,

∣xn+1−xn∣=∣(xn2+14)−(xn−12+14)∣=∣xn2−xn−12∣=∣xn+xn−1∣⋅∣xn−xn−1∣.|x_{n+1} - x_n| = \left|\left(x_n^2+\tfrac14\right) - \left(x_{n-1}^2+\tfrac14\right)\right| = |x_n^2 - x_{n-1}^2| = |x_n + x_{n-1}|\cdot|x_n - x_{n-1}|.∣xn+1​−xn​∣=​(xn2​+41​)−(xn−12​+41​)​=∣xn2​−xn−12​∣=∣xn​+xn−1​∣⋅∣xn​−xn−1​∣.

This has the right shape for the contractive inequality ∣xn+1−xn∣≤c ∣xn−xn−1∣|x_{n+1}-x_n| \le c\,|x_n-x_{n-1}|∣xn+1​−xn​∣≤c∣xn​−xn−1​∣, with ccc playing the role of ∣xn+xn−1∣|x_n+x_{n-1}|∣xn​+xn−1​∣.

Why This Does Not Establish Contractiveness

The contractive definition (Lecture 12) requires a single constant c∈(0,1)c \in (0,1)c∈(0,1) that works for every nnn. Here the coefficient ∣xn+xn−1∣|x_n+x_{n-1}|∣xn​+xn−1​∣ genuinely depends on nnn — it is not a fixed number. Although Step 2 above shows xn<12x_n < \tfrac12xn​<21​ for all nnn, so xn+xn−1<1x_n + x_{n-1} < 1xn​+xn−1​<1 strictly, the sequence increases toward 12\tfrac1221​, meaning xn+xn−1→1x_n + x_{n-1} \to 1xn​+xn−1​→1 as n→∞n\to\inftyn→∞. No fixed c<1c < 1c<1 can bound this ratio for all sufficiently large nnn, since the coefficients themselves approach 111. Hence this sequence, despite converging, is not contractive in the technical sense — a useful reminder that boundedness of consecutive-difference ratios is not enough; contractiveness demands a ratio bounded away from 111 by a fixed margin.

Question 2: Submultiplicativity of Limsup for Positive Sequences

This result parallels the Subadditivity of Limsup proved in the previous lecture, with the sum replaced by a product — and the proof follows the identical tailwise strategy, now using that both sequences are positive to justify multiplying inequalities.

Submultiplicativity of Limsup

Let (an)(a_n)(an​) and (bn)(b_n)(bn​) be bounded sequences of positive real numbers. Then

lim sup⁡n→∞(anbn)  ≤  (lim sup⁡n→∞an)(lim sup⁡n→∞bn).\limsup_{n\to\infty}(a_nb_n) \;\le\; \left(\limsup_{n\to\infty} a_n\right)\left(\limsup_{n\to\infty} b_n\right).n→∞limsup​(an​bn​)≤(n→∞limsup​an​)(n→∞limsup​bn​).
Proof

As in the previous lecture, write the limsups as limits of tail suprema:

lim sup⁡an=lim⁡n→∞sna,sna:=sup⁡{ak:k≥n};lim sup⁡bn=lim⁡n→∞snb,snb:=sup⁡{bk:k≥n};\limsup a_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} s_n^a, \quad s_n^a := \sup\{a_k : k\ge n\}; \qquad \limsup b_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} s_n^b, \quad s_n^b := \sup\{b_k : k\ge n\};limsupan​=n→∞lim​sna​,sna​:=sup{ak​:k≥n};limsupbn​=n→∞lim​snb​,snb​:=sup{bk​:k≥n}; lim sup⁡(anbn)=lim⁡n→∞snab,snab:=sup⁡{akbk:k≥n}.\limsup(a_nb_n) = \lim_{n\to\infty} s_n^{ab}, \quad s_n^{ab} := \sup\{a_kb_k : k\ge n\}.limsup(an​bn​)=n→∞lim​snab​,snab​:=sup{ak​bk​:k≥n}.

Key pointwise inequality. Fix nnn. For any k≥nk \ge nk≥n, ak≤snaa_k \le s_n^aak​≤sna​ and bk≤snbb_k \le s_n^bbk​≤snb​. Since ak,bk>0a_k, b_k > 0ak​,bk​>0, multiplying these inequalities preserves them:

akbk≤sna⋅snbfor every k≥n.a_kb_k \le s_n^a \cdot s_n^b \qquad \text{for every } k \ge n.ak​bk​≤sna​⋅snb​for every k≥n.

So sna⋅snbs_n^a \cdot s_n^bsna​⋅snb​ is an upper bound for {akbk:k≥n}\{a_kb_k : k\ge n\}{ak​bk​:k≥n}; taking the supremum of the left side over k≥nk \ge nk≥n gives

snab≤sna⋅snbfor every n.s_n^{ab} \le s_n^a \cdot s_n^b \qquad \text{for every } n.snab​≤sna​⋅snb​for every n.

Passing to the limit. Both (sna)(s_n^a)(sna​) and (snb)(s_n^b)(snb​) converge (they are nonincreasing and bounded below, since (an),(bn)(a_n),(b_n)(an​),(bn​) are bounded — Lecture 11), so the Product Limit Law (Lecture 7) applies:

lim⁡n→∞snab≤lim⁡n→∞(sna⋅snb)=(lim⁡n→∞sna)(lim⁡n→∞snb),\lim_{n\to\infty} s_n^{ab} \le \lim_{n\to\infty}\left(s_n^a \cdot s_n^b\right) = \left(\lim_{n\to\infty} s_n^a\right)\left(\lim_{n\to\infty} s_n^b\right),n→∞lim​snab​≤n→∞lim​(sna​⋅snb​)=(n→∞lim​sna​)(n→∞lim​snb​),

i.e. lim sup⁡(anbn)≤(lim sup⁡an)(lim sup⁡bn)\limsup(a_nb_n) \le (\limsup a_n)(\limsup b_n)limsup(an​bn​)≤(limsupan​)(limsupbn​).

Why positivity matters here

Multiplying an inequality ak≤snaa_k \le s_n^aak​≤sna​ by bkb_kbk​ only preserves the direction when bk≥0b_k \ge 0bk​≥0 — this is exactly the Multiplication and Order theorem from Lecture 3. Without the positivity hypothesis, the argument breaks down at the step akbk≤sna⋅snba_kb_k \le s_n^a\cdot s_n^bak​bk​≤sna​⋅snb​, and indeed the inequality can fail for sequences with mixed signs.

Question 3: A Sequence with Distinct Subsequential Limits

This example applies the Divergence Criteria of Lecture 9 (two subsequences with different limits ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ divergence), reformulated using the limsup/liminf language of Lectures 11 and 14.

Consider

xn=1+(−1)nn2n2−n+1.x_n = 1 + (-1)^n \frac{n^2}{n^2-n+1}.xn​=1+(−1)nn2−n+1n2​.
Even and Odd Subsequences

Even indices (n=2mn=2mn=2m): since (−1)2m=1(-1)^{2m}=1(−1)2m=1,

x2m=1+(2m)2(2m)2−2m+1→m→∞1+1=2,x_{2m} = 1 + \frac{(2m)^2}{(2m)^2-2m+1} \xrightarrow[m\to\infty]{} 1 + 1 = 2,x2m​=1+(2m)2−2m+1(2m)2​m→∞​1+1=2,

since (2m)2(2m)2−2m+1→1\dfrac{(2m)^2}{(2m)^2-2m+1} \to 1(2m)2−2m+1(2m)2​→1 by the Limit Comparison ideas of Lecture 13 (both numerator and denominator are dominated by their leading term 4m24m^24m2).

Odd indices (n=2m+1n=2m+1n=2m+1): since (−1)2m+1=−1(-1)^{2m+1}=-1(−1)2m+1=−1,

x2m+1=1−(2m+1)2(2m+1)2−(2m+1)+1→m→∞1−1=0,x_{2m+1} = 1 - \frac{(2m+1)^2}{(2m+1)^2-(2m+1)+1} \xrightarrow[m\to\infty]{} 1 - 1 = 0,x2m+1​=1−(2m+1)2−(2m+1)+1(2m+1)2​m→∞​1−1=0,

by the same reasoning applied to the odd-indexed subsequence.

Since these two subsequences converge to different limits (2≠02 \ne 02=0), the sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) does not converge, by the Divergence Criteria of Lecture 9. Moreover,

lim sup⁡n→∞xn=2,lim inf⁡n→∞xn=0,\limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n = 2, \qquad \liminf_{n\to\infty} x_n = 0,n→∞limsup​xn​=2,n→∞liminf​xn​=0,

consistent with the Convergence Criterion of Lecture 10: convergence would require these two quantities to agree.

Continuous Functions on Intervals

We now turn to a new topic: how continuity interacts with closed, bounded intervals. The two theorems below — the Boundedness Theorem and the Min-Max Theorem — both work by the same underlying mechanism: extract a sequence approaching the “bad” behavior (unboundedness, or the supremum), use Bolzano–Weierstrass (Lecture 10) to pass to a convergent subsequence, then use continuity to transport that convergence to the function values.

Bounded and Unbounded Functions

Bounded Function

A function f:A→Rf : A \to \mathbb{R}f:A→R is bounded on AAA if there exists M>0M > 0M>0 such that

∣f(x)∣≤Mfor all x∈A.|f(x)| \le M \qquad \text{for all } x \in A.∣f(x)∣≤Mfor all x∈A.

Just as with sequences (Lecture 7), it is useful to phrase the negation sequentially rather than via the raw ∀M\forall M∀M statement.

Unbounded Function

A function f:A→Rf : A \to \mathbb{R}f:A→R is not bounded on AAA if there exists a sequence (xn)⊂A(x_n) \subset A(xn​)⊂A such that

lim⁡n→∞∣f(xn)∣=∞.\lim_{n\to\infty} |f(x_n)| = \infty.n→∞lim​∣f(xn​)∣=∞.

The Boundedness Theorem

The theorem below is the reason closed, bounded intervals are special: continuity alone is not enough to guarantee boundedness on an arbitrary set (as the examples following the proof show), but it is enough on [a,b][a,b][a,b].

Boundedness Theorem

Let I=[a,b]I = [a,b]I=[a,b] be a closed and bounded interval, and let f:I→Rf : I \to \mathbb{R}f:I→R be continuous on III. Then fff is bounded on III.

Proof

Suppose, toward a contradiction, that fff is not bounded on III. Then by the definition above, there exists a sequence (xn)⊂I(x_n) \subset I(xn​)⊂I with lim⁡n→∞∣f(xn)∣=∞\lim_{n\to\infty}|f(x_n)| = \inftylimn→∞​∣f(xn​)∣=∞.

Since III is bounded and (xn)⊂I(x_n) \subset I(xn​)⊂I, the sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is itself bounded. By the Bolzano–Weierstrass Theorem (Lecture 10), there exists a convergent subsequence (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) with xnk→x∗x_{n_k} \to x^*xnk​​→x∗ for some x∗x^*x∗. Since III is closed, x∗∈Ix^* \in Ix∗∈I (a closed interval contains all its limit points, by the Interval Classification of Lecture 5).

Since fff is continuous on III and x∗∈Ix^* \in Ix∗∈I, fff is continuous at x∗x^*x∗. By the sequential criterion for continuity, xnk→x∗x_{n_k} \to x^*xnk​​→x∗ implies

lim⁡k→∞f(xnk)=f(x∗).\lim_{k\to\infty} f(x_{n_k}) = f(x^*).k→∞lim​f(xnk​​)=f(x∗).

But (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) is a subsequence of (xn)(x_n)(xn​), and ∣f(xn)∣→∞|f(x_n)| \to \infty∣f(xn​)∣→∞ forces ∣f(xnk)∣→∞|f(x_{n_k})| \to \infty∣f(xnk​​)∣→∞ as well (a subsequence of a properly divergent sequence is properly divergent — Lecture 13). This contradicts f(xnk)→f(x∗)∈Rf(x_{n_k}) \to f(x^*) \in \mathbb{R}f(xnk​​)→f(x∗)∈R, a finite value.

Hence fff must be bounded on III.

Why Every Hypothesis Is Necessary

Each of the following shows the Boundedness Theorem fails if one hypothesis is dropped:

  • f(x)=1/xf(x) = 1/xf(x)=1/x on (0,1](0,1](0,1]: continuous, but the interval is not closed (missing 000), and fff is unbounded near 000.
  • f(x)=xf(x) = xf(x)=x on [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞): continuous, but the interval is not bounded, and fff is unbounded.
  • f(x)=1/∣x∣f(x) = 1/|x|f(x)=1/∣x∣ for x∈[−1,1]∖{0}x \in [-1,1]\setminus\{0\}x∈[−1,1]∖{0}, with f(0)=0f(0) = 0f(0)=0: defined on a closed, bounded interval, but fff is not continuous at 000, and fff is unbounded near 000.

In each case, exactly one hypothesis of the theorem is missing, and boundedness fails as a result — confirming that closedness, boundedness of the interval, and continuity are all essential.

Absolute Maxima and Minima

Boundedness alone only guarantees f(I)f(I)f(I) has a supremum and infimum (by completeness) — it does not guarantee these values are actually attained by fff at some point of III. The next definition isolates this stronger property.

Absolute Maximum and Minimum

Let f:A→Rf : A \to \mathbb{R}f:A→R. Then fff has an absolute maximum on AAA if there exists x∗∈Ax^* \in Ax∗∈A such that

f(x)≤f(x∗)for all x∈A,f(x) \le f(x^*) \qquad \text{for all } x \in A,f(x)≤f(x∗)for all x∈A,

and an absolute minimum on AAA if there exists x∗∈Ax_* \in Ax∗​∈A such that

f(x)≥f(x∗)for all x∈A.f(x) \ge f(x_*) \qquad \text{for all } x \in A.f(x)≥f(x∗​)for all x∈A.
Relation to Supremum and Infimum

These conditions say exactly that

f(x∗)=sup⁡f(A),f(x∗)=inf⁡f(A).f(x^*) = \sup f(A), \qquad f(x_*) = \inf f(A).f(x∗)=supf(A),f(x∗​)=inff(A).

In particular, having an absolute maximum/minimum is strictly stronger than f(A)f(A)f(A) merely having a supremum/infimum in R\mathbb{R}R (guaranteed by completeness whenever f(A)f(A)f(A) is bounded) — it additionally requires that value to be achieved by some point of the domain, rather than merely approached.

The Min-Max Theorem

Combining the Boundedness Theorem with the “subsequence approaching the supremum” construction from the previous lecture’s quiz review, we now show that continuous functions on closed, bounded intervals do attain both an absolute maximum and minimum.

Min-Max Theorem

Let I=[a,b]I = [a,b]I=[a,b] be a closed, bounded interval and let f:I→Rf : I \to \mathbb{R}f:I→R be continuous on III. Then fff has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on III.

Proof

By the Boundedness Theorem, f(I)f(I)f(I) is bounded. By completeness (Lecture 3–4), sup⁡f(I)\sup f(I)supf(I) and inf⁡f(I)\inf f(I)inff(I) exist in R\mathbb{R}R. We show the supremum is attained; the infimum follows by the identical argument applied to −f-f−f.

Constructing an approximating sequence. For each n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N, since sup⁡f(I)−1n\sup f(I) - \tfrac1nsupf(I)−n1​ is not an upper bound of f(I)f(I)f(I) (Supremum Characterization, Lecture 4), there exists xn∈Ix_n \in Ixn​∈I with

sup⁡f(I)−1n≤f(xn)≤sup⁡f(I).\sup f(I) - \frac1n \le f(x_n) \le \sup f(I).supf(I)−n1​≤f(xn​)≤supf(I).

By the Squeeze Theorem, lim⁡n→∞f(xn)=sup⁡f(I)\lim_{n\to\infty} f(x_n) = \sup f(I)limn→∞​f(xn​)=supf(I) — this is exactly the “subsequence approaching the supremum” construction from the previous lecture, applied here to the values f(xn)f(x_n)f(xn​) rather than to a sequence directly.

Extracting a convergent subsequence of the domain points. Since (xn)⊂I(x_n) \subset I(xn​)⊂I and III is bounded, Bolzano–Weierstrass gives a subsequence (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) with xnk→x∗x_{n_k} \to x^*xnk​​→x∗ for some x∗x^*x∗; since III is closed, x∗∈Ix^* \in Ix∗∈I.

Transporting the limit via continuity. By the sequential criterion for continuity, f(xnk)→f(x∗)f(x_{n_k}) \to f(x^*)f(xnk​​)→f(x∗) as k→∞k \to \inftyk→∞. But (f(xnk))(f(x_{n_k}))(f(xnk​​)) is a subsequence of (f(xn))(f(x_n))(f(xn​)), which converges to sup⁡f(I)\sup f(I)supf(I) — so by the Subsequence Theorem (Lecture 9), (f(xnk))(f(x_{n_k}))(f(xnk​​)) must converge to the same limit:

f(x∗)=lim⁡k→∞f(xnk)=sup⁡f(I).f(x^*) = \lim_{k\to\infty} f(x_{n_k}) = \sup f(I).f(x∗)=k→∞lim​f(xnk​​)=supf(I).

Hence x∗∈Ix^* \in Ix∗∈I is a point at which fff attains its supremum, so fff has an absolute maximum at x∗x^*x∗.

The shared architecture of both theorems

Both the Boundedness Theorem and the Min-Max Theorem follow the exact same three-step template: (1) construct a sequence in III witnessing the extreme behavior (unboundedness, or approach to the supremum), (2) invoke Bolzano–Weierstrass to extract a convergent subsequence landing inside III — using closedness — and (3) invoke sequential continuity to transport that convergence to the function values, producing either a contradiction or the desired maximum. This is the standard method for proving existence results about continuous functions on closed, bounded intervals, and it will reappear when studying uniform continuity and the Intermediate Value Theorem.
